column2: You have a web page inside a folder named: prjHTML. Inside of this folder there is a sub-folder named: graphic. And, inside the graphic folder is a file named: background.jpg. What is the correct path you should use in the element of a web page located inside the prjHTML folder?

column5: You have to tell the browser to look in the graphic sub-folder. This tells the browser to look in the same folder that the web page is in.
Naming conventions. UPPER/lower case must match the actual filename.
Naming conventions. UPPER/lower case must match the actual filename.

The slash in the front tells the browser to start at the root directory. This could be several folders up from the prjHTML directory on the system. Only under special conditions do you want to start a filename path using the /"."
Correct!

You want the double dot ..". The single dot "." tells the browser to look in the current directory."

column3: c.
column5: This statement tells the browser to look in the current folder (which is people) for a sub-folder named graphic. This sub-folder is up one level inside of the parent folder prjHTML. You might want to draw this folder structure out so you can visualize what is happening.

column3: d.
column5: You started the path statement with a slash /" which tells the browser to go all the way up to the root directory. This would be at the very top of the file structure and probably doesn't contain a graphic sub-folder. Don't start your path statements with a forward slash!"

column3: e.
column5: This tells the browser to look in the same folder for a file named anneGreen.jpg. You have to go up one directory to prjHTML and THEN look for the graphic sub-folder.

column3: f.
column5: This statement tells the browser to look in the current folder (which is people) for a sub-folder named prjHTML and then for a sub-sub folder called graphic. Instead you want to go up one level to prjHTML and then into the graphic sub-folder. You might want to draw this folder structure out so you can visualize what is happening.

column2: The URL is: http://webExplorations.com/graphic/background/navBlueGradient.png. The http://" part of the URL is the ________________."

column5: The protocol tells the server how this particular request is to be deciphered. Other options include https:// (secure web page request) file:// (file on the local system) and ftp:// (file transfer protocol).

The domain is the specific name of the server where the file being requested should be found. This translates into an IP address (The IP for Google.com was: 216.239.51.99). Each server on the Internet has its own unique domain name/IP address.

column3: path of folders and sub-folders on the server
column5: When a file is located inside a series of subfolders that is called the path. This is the path" that the server must follow to locate the specific file on the server."

column3: path of folders and sub-folders on the client machine
column5: If a request is sent to http:// or https:// then the series of folders is on the server. If the request is sent to file:// then this path would be on the client machine, the local hard drive.

The filename is the name of the specific file that is being requested. If there is no filename then the server will display the index.html file. If an index.html file is not available then a list of files and folders at that location is shown in the browser.
